Welcome to the Make Peace Business System. This is an exciting moment. You're about to discover the secrets that have made Clayton Make Peace one of the most sought after copywriters and direct response marketers in the world. Secrets that will show you how you can be more successful and make more money than you ever thought possible. We're not talking obscure theories that may or may not produce results. What Clayton reveals is a proven system. One you can easily adapt to your own business and style. That has produced real, measurable results time after time. One that will enable you to land your dream clients, produce bigger winners more often, and collect those big royalty checks you've always dreamed about. On the first two CDs, seasoned copywriter and marketer Tony Flores gets Clayton to introduce the Make Peace Business System and explain why it's so different from everything out there. Why it works and why you'd have to be certifiably insane not to consider adopting it for yourself. If you like, you can print out the transcript to keep handy while you're listening to the interview. You'll find it on the bonus CD in your case along with all the other transcripts, PDFs, and special reports. Now, let's join Tony and Clayton. Hello. Hey Clayton. Hey John. How you doing? How's it going? Well, it's good to have you. As you know, the reason I had you give me a call today is to discuss this model that you've been writing about, prepared a series of what I believe to be very tough and questions about this, really getting into more of the specifics and some of the things that I'm just not sure of and haven't seen with my own two eyes and so I'd like to hear more from you about it. Since I have 500 friends listening into the call right now, why don't you begin with a quick explanation? A quick explanation of how it works. Sure. Well, essentially, most copywriters make money in one of three ways. Actually, there are four ways in all. I'll just go through them all. The first one is you can get a job. You go through a course, you get your jobs, and you apply for a job, maybe with an ad agency or a company that does direct response. Len Bailey is on the phone with us today and he was, until he came to work for us, he was writing copy for a major coin company in New Hampshire. And so, you know, there are agencies. I hired a guy out of a radio station once and mentored him and now he owns a multi-million dollar company in New Orleans. But anyway, you know, you can get these kinds of jobs and they're really great incubators, especially when you're starting out. I mean, I did that myself. Yahoo says that the average salary for a copywriter in America is around $90,000. And I'm assuming that they weren't polling people like me and Gary Benzavanga and Jim Rutz. When they came up with that number, it's more likely they were polling people who had regular jobs as copywriters. So, you know, $90,000, you know, if you rise to the top at a major agency, you'll make much more than that, definitely into six figures. But if you're just starting out, you may start at $40,000 or $35,000 or $30,000, you know. But that's one way to go. And if you're into job security and if you don't mind going to work every day and dealing with a boss and everything, that's one way, okay? Then the way that most people, including AWAI, refer to copywriting is in terms of freelancing. And freelancing is where you... Most freelancers just work out of their home and they write for a lot of different clients during the year. And those copywriters range from six figures up to a million dollars a year in income. And they receive their income from a combination of advances, flat fees and royalties on the work that they do. The royalty writers obviously are the ones at the upper end of that scale. The downside to freelancing though, there are several. Number one, there's only so much time in the year that you can set aside for writing. And each time you go to work on a new product or for a new client, you have to go through a learning curve. You have to learn about the product, the market, what they've tried before, what worked, what didn't. And that takes time. And you don't get paid for that time. That's part of the writing process. And you also have to spend time selling yourself to new clients all the time. When I was freelancing, I was able to do maybe an average of eight promotions a year. And I would schedule myself out in advance. And I had to spend time sending emails out to potential clients, spending time with them on the phone and schmoozing and doing all of that stuff that none of us really enjoy doing. And it takes even more time away from writing. You got a hustle a little bit. Yeah, you got a hustle. And the fact is, I got to the point where I enjoyed it over time, but especially at first, it was intimidating. It was frustrating. And especially when you're under the gun and need to finish a project or generate some money to spend this month. And instead, you're banging the phones trying to find work for next month. It can be pretty disruptive and frustrating. The third drawback is that every client you work with knows that next month he'll be working for another client and in many cases, a direct competitor of theirs. And so the chance is that they're going to give you strategic information that they consider to be the company's crown jewels about what they've tested, what they've tried, what lists are going to, their prospect profiles, their average sale, things like that. The chances that they're going to freely share that information with you are fairly limited. Over time, they may learn to trust you and you may get more information. But I mean, as recently as two years ago, I was meeting with a client that I had been doing business with for 15 years. And I asked a question about one of their promotions. And this is right after they said, oh, we'll tell you anything. We trust you. And I asked a question about one of their promotions and there was this long silence and then the creative director says, we're not at liberty to share that. And so, you know, regardless of what they tell you, they are not giving you all of the information you need to create the biggest possible winner. And then finally, there's competition on each assignment and it really comes in two ways. Number one, it's an established procedure that when these companies hire a copywriter to beat a control, they quite often will hire two or three writers at a time. And then mail all three of those writers' efforts against the control in a future mailing. So instead of just trying to beat the control, which is a known quantity, you can look at it and you can say, OK, well, here are the weak points, here are the places where I would beat it. You're also up against the unknown quantity of one, two, even three new packages by other copywriters. And depending on where you are in your career, you know, it would be pretty daunting to find out that, especially after the fact, as often happens, that while you thought you were going up against a single control in which you had spotted weaknesses, you were also in business to you going up against Arthur Johnson or Jim Rods or Paris Lampropolis on that project. And so your odds of winning obviously are much lower. And then even if you're lucky enough to get the control in a situation like that, that client next month is going to go out and hire Arthur Johnson, Jim Rods, Paris Lampropolis, Carlene, Anglick, Cole, or another top writer to try to beat your control and to get a better response rate, average sale and return on investment. And so that means that even when you get the control, there's a very good chance that the amount of royalties you'll earn from that control will be very limited because every time they remail it or every time they use it online, they'll be testing you against someone else. And then the third option, which is really kind of what I fell into back in the 1980s, is what I call partnering. And it differs from freelancing because it focuses on creating a deeper relationship with just one or two clients. It eliminates the learning curve. It eliminates the time you waste selling yourself during the year. It gives you full access to the company's crown jewels. It eliminates competition because you have a two-way exclusive with that client. And it allows you to create up to seven streams of income from a single client. That model, combined with what I think is some fairly decent copywriting, is what has gotten my income range up to four times higher than my competitors, than other A-level writers. And so we still don't know for sure yet because I haven't seen my tax return, but I'm pretty sure that last year we did right around four million, maybe slightly less, maybe slightly more, but four million in royalties. So that's the difference. It was easier for me because I was only dealing with a fraction of the number of personalities I otherwise would have had to deal with. I was able to focus on one product and get to know the product and the market intimately. I didn't have to spend one minute during the year selling myself to anybody or filling my schedule. And I had advantages that no freelancer has in terms of protection from competition and full data, past test data, everything that client knew about his market. By far the biggest money you can make, of course, though, isn't partnering. It's really the next step. And that would be building your own business, selecting a product and just taking it to the moon yourself. That way, instead of making a percentage of revenues and profits, you make 100% of the profits. It's a great way to go. The other day on our blog, the guy that invented Trade Station, I think it was, no, Invest Tools. Yeah, we got on there and he said, you know, make these is right about partnering, but there is a better way. And, you know, he's right in a way. Building your own business is a better way. That's how you get to be, you know, Billy Mays or Gutty Ranker or Ron Popeil. Right. George Sugarman. But you have to, I mean, reality intervenes. First of all, although your income potential is unlimited, so is the downside. You know, the direct marketing world is littered with the corpses of people who tried this, didn't have enough capital, or weren't able to really pull out a big winner. And so, you know, building your own business, it's easier now online where costs are lower, but still you have to eat while you're building the company. So, you know, you have to have some capital reserves there. And so I think for most of the people that read the total package, most of the people that I talked to, partnering is by far the most potentially profitable and also the most realistic way to begin ramping up your income right now today. So, you know, that's the basic concept. One of the things that, you know, you describe basically how you make, you know, up to four times more than a lot of the other very, very high level freelancers, but how does that work out for the business owner? Why would he agree to give out that much more compensation? Yeah, it sounds like it'd be a pretty tough sell. You walk in and you say, I know you're paying your freelancers a million dollars a year right now, but if you hire me exclusively and promise never to hire them again, you'll only have to pay me four. But it really doesn't work that way. You know, first of all, the scarcest, I've said this so many times, but the scarcest resource every direct response business owner has is great copywriting. If you try to get on Arthur Johnson's dance card, chances are he'll just probably tell you no. Or if he does say yes, his closest writing slot could be six months a year or even more from now. The same's true with Paris and Carline and all of these other top freelance copywriters. And that's a major problem for a company that needs to increase its customer base today or maximize revenues from that customer base today, or to take advantage of an opportunity or launch a new product that's going to be ready next month or even the month after. So having instant access to a competent copywriter is money in the bank for a direct response company, and it solves a major problem that they have right now. Excuse me, the second reason is that right now the companies that you're talking to have a pool of ideas for products and for promotions that have been generated by their staff or by the company owner himself. But when a direct marketing pro, a copywriter who understands marketing nuts and bolts, is added to the mix, somebody who's really gone to school on copywriting and offers and headlines and the products that are working best in the mail and online right now and so forth. And somebody walks through the door with that capacity and then adds to it an intimate understanding of the client's company and his product and his market. The pool of great ideas is going to increase exponentially. So in other words, you bring a lot to the table. Great ideas are really the fuel that runs direct marketing companies. And so having an exclusive with a company and being completely committed to that company does that. Also, I have more ideas, great ideas for my clients in the shower in the morning than I do when I come to work and sit down at my desk. The thing is that when you're committed to a client, you're constantly thinking about that client and everything else you see is in the context of that client's company. So if you see a new piece of software or a new web technology or a new format for a direct mail piece, that client is going to get that idea. He knows that you're not going to take it down to the road, down the road to his competitor. And then third, there are more opportunities that can be seized. Like I said, there are so many, I mean, every direct response company out there is like a giant sieve and it's just like it's got money just pouring out the bottom of it because of all of the holes in its marketing plan. And having the horsepower of a dedicated copywriter marketing and marketer on board gives you the opportunity to patch those leaks and to realize that revenue and those profits that you're losing right now. So, you know, every company that you talk to is going to say, you know, we've got more ideas and Carter has little liver pills, but our problem is execution. We can't find enough copywriters. We can't find enough marketing people to take advantage of them. So, and as we just said, you're going to come in with even more ideas. So there's huge growth potential there. Well, I won't actually rewind a second because you just you mentioned a couple things in terms of seizing opportunities and better results and profits and everything. And none of them are for me to really believe that and especially to believe that across the board. I think I just need a little more details or specifics. I'm not totally convinced of why that is until I can get a little bit more. Could you go into that just a little bit more deeply so that I know exactly what you mean there? Sure. Well, both in terms of better ideas and more opportunities. One of the things that I think copywriters should do, you know, when they're when they're dealing with a client on, let's say a client hires you to create a new customer acquisition promotion. Well, you know, as part of your quote research for that, you know, you it's very plausible to ask, Well, you know, I'd really like to see what happens to the to the new customers that I'm going to produce for you. You know, what their experience is going to be, how they need to be set up for a secondary sale and so forth. And while you're doing that, you're going to see all kinds of situations in the company that aren't being addressed. I mean, in a major $100 million direct marketing company I was working with, for example, this comes under the better ideas banner. Okay. I've been working with the company for a couple of months and I just on a on a whim, I thought, you know, my direct mail is working pretty well. But I wonder, you know, whether customer service is helping or hurting hurting me. So I picked up the phone, I called the toll free number and I said, I want to subscribe to your newsletter. And the woman says, Okay, how long of a subscription would you like? And I said, Well, what do you got? And she goes, Well, you know, one year is $99 and two years is $189. And I said, Well, okay, so one year is slightly cheaper on a monthly basis. Or it's about the same price. And I said, you know, what's to keep me from just subscribing for 99 and then subscribing again next year for 99 if I like it. She goes, nothing. And I said, Well, you know, do I get my money back if I don't, you know, if I subscribe for two years? Well, yeah. Okay, well, are there any pre, you know, I had to drag it out of her. And then finally I go, Okay, well, you know what, this sounds really good. But you told me that I can only subscribe for one or two years. I think I'd like to try three years. What's your offer on that? And her answer, my hand to God was, I'm sorry, sir, we don't offer three year subscriptions. Well, you know, that resulted in an email that was fired off in about 30 seconds. The company owner that says, Hey, you know what, here's an idea. Why don't you get some smarter customer service people or at least script them better? You know, and we did that and we saw a noticeable increase in average in her to sale on our direct direct mail promotions as a result. More opportunities seized another company I walked into and we were doing great customer acquisition promotions. And but I started noticing when I was looking at his RFA tables, his recency RFA tables are for those of you who don't know, it's simply a spreadsheet that shows how many of his customers are at various levels of activity in the company. And one of them is, is their recency of purchase metric. And the recency of purchase metric tells you, you know, whether or not, you know, how many people have ordered within the last 30 days, within 31 to 60 days, and then so forth going all the way back. And the company was defining an active customer as somebody who had ordered within the last 12 months. And so I took a look at that and I said, Well, do you realize that you're going to lose 20% of your active, active customer base in the next 60 days? Because they're either at 11 or 12 months since their last purchase. And the company owner said, Yeah, yeah, I know that. He said, We've been meaning to get around to do a customer retention program, but we just don't have the time and we don't have the copywriters available to do that for us. And here a fifth of his company was about to vanish into thin air. Okay. So, you know, being committed to the company would enable you to say, Look, don't worry about that. We can't let this happen. I will write a promotion or a series to retain those customers. And, you know, we'll keep them on board. We'll turn them from 12 month customers into one month customers on their recency metric. And you'll retain them so that you'll have net growth this month instead of a net loss in terms of active customers. And you're saying that you're motivated to do that because you're on a percentage deal with that client, whereas if you're just getting paid by the job and you don't really care. Well, you're motivated on as far as a deal that you strike for that kind of a promotion, there's any number of ways you could go. But let's say, for example, that you just have a very simple deal with a client where you're doing his new customer acquisition promotions for a royalty, you know, I'll say, you know, $20, $30 per thousand pieces mailed or online a royalty of 5 to 10% of revenues. Okay. And in addition, though, you're also doing back end monthly promotions to existing customers. Right. And this is another big advantage to business owners, by the way. Well, you know, if you're doing the back end promotions, you don't have to charge an arm and a leg to do anything that is going to either retain customers or increases customer base. Because the bigger that base is, the more money you're going to make because you're doing the promotions to that base and you're taking a percentage of sales. So it gives you the flexibility as a writer to say, which I do quite often, as a matter of fact, hey, don't worry about this one. I'll do it free because within 30 days, my income is going to jump 20 or 30% once we implement this retention program. You know, and so it gives you a lot more flexibility there, too. And of course, business owners love getting anything they're not paying for. And don't we all, right? And then, you know, the bottom line really is better results, faster growth and greater profits. The key is, of course, showing or demonstrating, creating personal proof for the business owner that you can indeed deliver those things. But, you know, if he has instant access to a copywriter to take advantage of opportunities, if he's got a bigger pool of better ideas because you're involved, and if he's able to seize more opportunities that he's missing right now, and believe me, as a company owner, when you see those opportunities month after month after month not being taken, not being capitalized on, it's torture. It's horrible. I mean, that's money right off your bottom line. And so that's a huge benefit. So those are just some of the reasons. I might also say, as more and more people become aware of this model and business owners look around at companies, including, in some cases, their competitors, who are partnering and see the results that partnering produces, I think you're going to see company owners actively seeking and recruiting copywriter marketers as partners. Well, you know, a lot of what you say makes sense, but I worry that it makes sense for you, not necessarily for me, or I think a lot of the other people that are listening to you on the call. You know, you obviously have a very extensive track record that you can do all these things, and you're a famous copywriter, and that you're already heavily in demand. And so, you know, obviously a client sees, you know, Green when he's talking to you, whereas it may not be the case for somebody like me, or I think somebody on the other people on our list at this stage. So how does that work if you're not famous? Or if you don't have any, you know, monster winners under your belt, like, where you've generated a million new customers and, you know, all that kind of stuff. What is the regular guy to do? Well, before I get to that, let me just, as you said, rewind for a second, because, you know, I gave you, you asked a question about specific examples of how the partnership model can really benefit company owners, and major opportunities that aren't fully exploited. And I gave you specific ones of that, you know, from my actual experiences, but let me just give you a couple more real quick, okay? Okay. Number one, the company owner is going to get more effective new customer acquisition promotions, because instead of working with a writer who has a passing interest in the company and who is just learning about the company, its products and its prospects, he now has somebody who's, you know, eating, sleeping and breathing customer attention, or a customer acquisition for him, and who's intimately aware and grows more intimately aware every month with every new promotion of what's working and what isn't, okay? Secondly, there's the opportunity to go in and look at things like his product stuffers and thank you promotions. You know, I'm very big on every single customer contact, and it is an opportunity to do two things, bond with a customer and sell something. And whether the sale is a bump or a cross marketing promotion, that's an area where most companies are doing a really horrible job. When they deliver their product, what's in it, you know, to set up a secondary sale, do they do a thank you letter that says, oh, by the way, you know, I'm going to give you an extra 30 days to increase your subscription from a one year to a two year. You know, there are tons of opportunities there, discount coupons, calendars with discount coupons, catalogs and so forth. And by the way, for copywriters, there's nothing sweeter than to write a website or a catalog in 2005 and in 2008 because it's standing copy, you're still getting royalties on it. More effective welcome aboard in Gauntlet series. We call Gauntlets the first 30 to 60 days typically that a new customer is on file. So during that period of time, a concerted effort to bond with a customer and to generate a secondary sale does miracles for the company because number one, they can allocate some of that money to their new customer acquisition campaigns, which allows them to be very aggressive in the mail or on TV or on radio or any media they want because they know that even though they may not get 100% of cost back on that initial promotion, once they have one of these kinds of programs in place for a period of time, they can count on secondary sales being at a certain level within the first 30, 60 or 90 days. So it's a very powerful tool for exploding companies customer bases. Then there's more effective promotions to existing customers. Again, now you know these customers are your friends. You're able to structure polls and surveys and even focus groups with them. You get to know what they want, what kinds of offers that they prefer, when they're most likely to order. You're able to segment the file and do both vertical and horizontal promotions to them. So that instead of everybody on the file receiving the same promotion, at the same time, some of the people on the, you know, you'll do one promotion in the first week of the month that's based on a shared experience like a holiday, you know, Christmas, Easter, Halloween, whatever. And then those are the vertical promotions. And then the next week you can do a horizontal promotion where you address each group of customers on the basis of some shared quality, their recency of order, their frequency of order, the average size of their order, and so forth, months on file, and so forth. And so by slicing and dicing a customer file that way, you maximize your revenue from every single segment of the file. And it's a multiplying effect. The growth in revenue's profits can just be, you know, staggering. I mentioned more effective customer retention. Another one is testing. You know, every company out there feels guilty as hell because they don't test enough. They know they should be testing more. They know they should be testing headlines, copy, offers, media, you know, there are 100 things to test and they're not doing it because they don't have the horsepower. They don't have the copywriters to create the test panels. And being a copywriter marketer who can not only come in and create test panels, but can also help strategize what we should be testing now. What's most important to test on our current control? Again, that's another quantum growth driver for a company. So those are just some of the other things I wanted to cover. In terms of specifics. Yeah, in terms of specifics. You know, Tony, that's your tremendous value to the total package. You force us to be specific. I have a whole idea behind this screaming eagle. I still think it was one of the most brilliant ideas I've heard. But anyway, yeah. Thank you. And one of the things, because I had asked you a question, you went back. We can get to that in a second. But one of the things that I feel like I hear you saying also with this, and I think it's partially an answer to a question, though not directly, is that part of this process is the appeal to the bit, or why it works, not so much because of the copywriter, it's because of the process, the system. Right, the system creates advantage. So I think as copywriters sometimes we tend to think that we do everything, but maybe in this case, the system is producing results on top of the copywriters. Is that what you're saying? That's correct. No, that's correct. The fact of the matter is if you can go in and just create a decent response in some of these areas, not even world beating, but you're bringing him more online with more tests and customer retention and bonding with existing customers, which results in greater sales down the road, and more retention down the road, too. So doing these things, even with okay copy, you can see a multiplying effect in the company. And just another point here, if you're a copywriter, marketer, and you're talking to a business owner about this, remember that you're first and foremost a salesperson. What does a salesperson do? What's the most important thing for a salesperson to be aware of? The dominant, resonant emotions of his prospect. Now your prospect is a company owner. How is he feeling about his current situation? I haven't met a business owner yet who wasn't frustrated that he was growing faster, who wasn't frustrated and feeling guilty that he wasn't doing a better job of testing. That was just agonizing over his cancellation rates, or agonizing over his low renewal rates. So those are the drivers that really put the business owner on your side in the first place. And if you can go in and show him how together as a team that two of you can take care of these problems and incrementally improve each one of them, the bottom line to the business owner is huge. Okay. Okay, now let's get back to what I was asking in terms of what a regular guy does, somebody who's not famous, who doesn't have a track record that's been blasted across the internet, all of that kind of stuff. We answered it a little bit, I think, in the sense that you're selling the process and not just selling yourself, you're selling the process itself. But tell me a little bit more about that because I really wonder how eager a business owner is going to be to jump on a deal like this if he hasn't heard of the person or they don't have, you know, a list of what examples are on my along. Right. Well, you know, again, this is like a first date. An example that I know that you're familiar with. Sure. You know, let's say you're going out on a first date with a gallon and you immediately show her a picture of what you hope is the outcome of that date, which could be anything from blissfully smoking a cigarette in the afterglow or marriage and children, you know, each of which could be equally terrifying to on a point. So I mean, I think that would be a fairly major blunder. And I think it's the same way with with a company that you're dealing with. You know, there is my buddy, Rich Sheffrin, is always making a big deal about the three levels of proof, you know, that there's there's some objective third party proof where you, you know, in a direct mail piece, for example, you'll site Bloomberg as the authority on the on a particular fact or point that you're trying to make that third party proof so and so says this and then there's there's social proof, which is far more powerful. And that's when a peer tells your prospect that he should buy your product. Okay. And then there's personal proof for it where your prospect himself experiences the power of what you are trying to get him to do. And so not only do you go in armed with understanding the dominant emotions of the company owner that you wanting to partner with, but you also under go in understanding that showing him a mental image of this person. And the two of you blissfully having that smoke or, you know, where you hope this thing will ultimately go could be a mistake. And then, and then secondly, you know, the best way to proceed really is to provide personal proof. Let him sample the goods, you know, and I actually call it a first date. And I think that's when I'm talking to a new client. They quite in fact, at this point in my career, though, quite often, you know, begin talking to me about partnering and, you know, building a long term exclusive relationship. And I quite often will tell them, No, you know what, let's not. Instead, let's let's test drive each other. Let's have a first date. Why don't we do a project together? And, you know, if I like the amount of money I make, and if you like the amount of money you make, and we both like working with each other, then we'll talk about the next step. And I think it's really the same no matter who you are, where you are in your career. Moving from freelancing into partnering really should be a natural process. And it shouldn't be a sales process, really, on either party's part. You want to know that you enjoy working together. You want to both feel that the process of creating the promotion was pleasant, stimulating and fun. And you both need to feel that the outcome was mutually satisfying. So, you know, I prefer, and I advise my friends, look, just, you know, do a project together, maybe do two projects together. Now, if the business owner is fairly unsuspecting and he just basically thinks he's hiring a freelancer, that's fine, too, you know. But during that process, ask questions that allow you to then make suggestions that demonstrate and provide personal proof to your prospective partner that you bring tremendous value to the table way beyond simply writing copy. And if you do that, then you don't need to be famous. You know, you don't need to have, you know, your own online easing, although they never heard, but you don't need to. You don't need to have 100 winners under your belt, because what you've done for other people in the past is far less salient to this prospective partner than what you did for him last month. And so, you know, that's what's going to ultimately not just convince a potential partner, but have a potential partner pursue you. You know, I've told this story before. You know, sometimes, at some point after almost three years of writing the total package, I've told every story I have four or five times. And so, I'm going to eventually, I think, get to the point where I just hold up a card with a number on it and everybody will know which story I'm referring to. Like the guy whose wife has heard all of his jokes before, you know. But when I started working with Security Rare Coin, and I'm not going to say stop me if you've heard this, because I know you have. When I first started working with Security Rare Coin, I was just doing customer acquisition and, you know, direct mail pieces, 16, 24-page sales letters and six by nine envelopes. We were doing really well. His subscriber file was growing like crazy. And he was very happy with that. So, he had personal proof that, okay, maybe this Clayton does know what he's talking about. And so, you know, he then approached me and he said, tell you what, I don't want to work with other copywriters and I don't want you to work with other investment companies, financial companies. Why don't we have an exclusive? And since it's a time I really didn't have anyone else I was working with, it's fine by me. So, we set up an arrangement on that base so I could focus on his customer acquisition. And so then one day, it was in December, I said, hey Bill, I said, what do you do with these new customers after they come on board? And he said, oh, well, they get their newsletter every month and there's a little stuffer in there that offers them a rare coin that they can buy. And that's really where we make our money. And I said, well, could I see some of those promotions? And he said, sure. And so I called him up on the phone and I say, hey Bill, these suck. These really stink, stinkin' up the joint. I think I could do a lot better. And he said, well, okay, they're working pretty good, but if you think you can, tell you what, why don't you write the insert for January? And I said, okay, cool. It was a two page insert. He said, how much are you going to charge me? And I said, nothing. I just want to see if I can do it. And he says, okay, because there's no price better than free. I mean, I guess I could offer to pay him for the privilege of writing it, but I didn't have to. And he said, well, I don't know what to do. He jumped it free. So I wrote the piece and it literally took me a day, less than a day probably, if I remember right. And I turned in the copy and he said, oh, well, this is different from what we're doing now. And I said, well, you expected the same. And I said, mail it. I went to the trouble to write it. Mail the damn thing. And he says, okay, fine. I will. And story short, his sales jumped a thousand percent in 30 days. He had been doing $360,000 a month in sales and we did 3.6 million in January based on that one insert. Well, you know, at that point, I didn't have to pitch anything to the guy. You know, he was on the phone to me and says, okay, that's it. I want you to move here. I want you to take over all my marketing. I want you to, you know, go on and he went on and on and on. And I said, Bill, Bill, you can't afford me. And at the time I was making, I was making $90,000 a year. And I said, Bill, of course, this was, you know, what, 27 years ago. But I was making about $90,000 a year. And I said, you can't afford me. And he said, try me. And I said, okay, I want, first of all, if I'm going to move my family, which I was willing to do, you don't have to do this. Okay. All of my clients now are long distance. But at the time I was kind of looking for a change anyway. So I said, okay, if I'm going to move my family up there, first of all, you got to pay all our moving expenses. And he goes done. And I said, and $40,000 bonus because I'm going to have to cancel projects with other clients. I said, done. And I said, and I want a quarter million dollar a year base pay retainer. He said done. And I said, and I want 5% of sales. And he said, so when can you be here? And I said, Oh, did I mention the company car, a Cadillac or better? And he goes, when can you be here? So of course, at that point, I'm kicking myself because I figured I could have probably asked for a double. But it was a sweat enough. Yeah. So basically, my point is, it was a natural process. I demonstrated my value first. And then it wasn't a matter of selling him. It was just simply a matter of setting the deal. I see. I see. Well, again, one of the things that I'm curious about or that doesn't quite make sense to me is that, you know, in writing the newsletter every month and just being here, I get the opportunities network with a lot of the other top writers and marketers in our industry and on the internet. And they're very, very sharp people, obviously. And one of the things that makes me wonder is if this is such a superior model or way of working a relationship model for both entrepreneurs and copywriters, why aren't more established kind of top writers doing it? Well, you know, change is tough, but more and more of them are. A few years ago, I helped Paraslan Propolis set up an exclusive partnering relationship with Soundview down in Atlanta, Chip Woods Group. And last year, Jim Pongri, who's one of the top writers in the health field, called me up and asked me to help him set up a partnering arrangement with Early to Rise. Last month, Rich Scheffrant is rich, who is desperately in need of copywriting help. And so I put him together with a friend of mine, Bob Hutchinson, and kind of convinced from the sidelines to help them structure a partnering relationship. Did you meet Bob at Rich's event, Tony? I did. He's a great guy, obviously. Yeah. Yeah, he's a hell of a copywriter. Yeah, he is. He's really good. And so those are guys that I've actually helped set up those things, and all of those are A-level writers. But in addition to them, since I began writing about this in January, if you look at the, go to the archives, look at the comments at the end of each issue, you'll see that there are other copywriters coming out of Woodwork. And with stories about how they're partnering, and some of them in response to the articles we've done, but also some of them have just kind of fallen into this naturally like I did with SRC. And so more and more people are doing it. And I think as word gets out, and I'm doing my best, I mean, I almost feel like a union organizer here. I'd like to organize all of the companies out there to say, no, we don't want to just work with freelancers, we want partners. And I want all of the copywriters to say, who needs freelancing, I want to partner with the companies I work with. And so I think as the word gets out more and more, more people are going to be doing it, and we'll see the model changing. Module one of the Make-Peace Business System. Clayton's interview with Tony Flores is continued on disc two.
